summary:
The Avignon Papacy (1309–1377 AD) was the period during which the papal court resided not in Rome but in Avignon in southern France (then technically under the suzerainty of the Kingdom of Naples but effectively under French influence), resulting from the struggle between Pope Boniface VIII and King Philip IV of France over clerical taxation and papal authority. Following Boniface's humiliating capture and death (1303), French cardinals dominated the College of Cardinals and elected the French bishop Clement V as pope; he moved the curia to Avignon in 1309.

The Avignon period, which Petrarch called the 'Babylonian Captivity of the Church,' saw seven consecutive French popes and a papacy that critics accused of being a tool of French royal policy. The Avignon court was actually enormously wealthy and administratively sophisticated — the popes built the massive Palais des Papes in Avignon, reorganized papal finances, and expanded the Church's bureaucratic apparatus. But the spiritual damage of an obviously France-dependent papacy was severe: it undermined papal claims to universal, above-national authority just as national monarchies were strengthening. The return of the papacy to Rome under Gregory XI (1377) was followed immediately by the catastrophic [[event:2295|Western Schism]] (1378–1417), when competing French and Italian claimants to the papacy produced decades of divided Church authority. The combined effect of the Avignon period and the Western Schism critically undermined papal prestige and authority, contributing to the spiritual atmosphere from which the Protestant Reformation would emerge in the following century.
